inputs.story: Waterford Spa had a full appointment calendar booked today. Help Janice figure out the schedule by matching each masseuse to her client, and determine the total price for each. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. Hannah paid more than Teri's client.
2. Freda paid 20 dollars more than Lynda's client.
3. Hannah paid 10 dollars less than Nancy's client.
4. Nancy's client, Hannah and Ginger were all different clients.
5. Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client.
inputs.constants:
Clients: Aimee; Ginger; Freda; Hannah.
Prices: $150; $160; $170; $180.
Masseuses: Lynda; Nancy; Teri; Whitney.
inputs.solution:
Clients	Prices	Masseuses
Aimee	$170	Nancy
Ginger	$150	Teri
Freda	$180	Whitney
Hannah	$160	Lynda

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

Here is the problem to be solved:
<problem_to_solve>
Waterford Spa had a full appointment calendar booked today. Help Janice figure out the schedule by matching each masseuse to her client, and determine the total price for each. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Hannah paid more than Teri's client.
2. Freda paid 20 dollars more than Lynda's client.
3. Hannah paid 10 dollars less than Nancy's client.
4. Nancy's client, Hannah and Ginger were all different clients.
5. Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client.

Constants:
Clients: Aimee; Ginger; Freda; Hannah.
Prices: $150; $160; $170; $180.
Masseuses: Lynda; Nancy; Teri; Whitney.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

ASP Program:
asp_generation.thinking:
Here's a thinking process:

1.  **Analyze the Problem:**
   - **Goal:** Match each masseuse to her client, and determine the total price for each.
   - **Categories:**
     - Clients: Aimee, Ginger, Freda, Hannah
     - Prices: $150, $160, $170, $180
     - Masseuses: Lynda, Nancy, Teri, Whitney
   - **Clues:**
     1. Hannah paid more than Teri's client.
     2. Freda paid 20 dollars more than Lynda's client.
     3. Hannah paid 10 dollars less than Nancy's client.
     4. Nancy's client, Hannah and Ginger were all different clients.
     5. Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client.
   - **Note:** The puzzle asks to match "each masseuse to her client, and determine the total price for each." This implies a relationship between Masseuse, Client, and Price. Wait, the clue says "Hannah paid more than...", "Freda paid...", etc. In logic grid puzzles, sometimes the names given as "Clients" are actually the ones who paid, meaning the "Client" category here refers to the person paying (the customer), and the "Masseuse" category refers to the employee. So we need to map Masseuse -> Client -> Price. Actually, the clue says "Hannah paid...", so Hannah is a client. "Teri's client" means the client of masseuse Teri. So we have a ternary relation: `schedule(Masseuse, Client, Price)`.
   - Let's verify the categories: Clients (Aimee, Ginger, Freda, Hannah), Prices (150, 160, 170, 180), Masseuses (Lynda, Nancy, Teri, Whitney).
   - We need to assign exactly one Client and one Price to each Masseuse.
   - The relationship predicate will be `assign(Masseuse, Client, Price)` or `match(Masseuse, Client, Price)`. I'll use `assign(M, C, P)`.

2.  **Extract Constants:**
   - Clients: "Aimee", "Ginger", "Freda", "Hannah"
   - Prices: 150, 160, 170, 180 (strip $)
   - Masseuses: "Lynda", "Nancy", "Teri", "Whitney"

3.  **Formulate ASP Rules (Mental Draft):**
   - **Constants:**
     ```asp
     client("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").
     price(150; 160; 170; 180).
     masseuse("Lynda"; "Nancy"; "Teri"; "Whitney").
     ```
   - **Search Space:**
     ```asp
     {assign(M, C, P): client(C), price(P)}=1 :- masseuse(M).
     ```
   - **Uniqueness:**
     ```asp
     {M1=M2; C1=C2; P1=P2}=0 :- assign(M1,C1,P1), assign(M2,C2,P2), (M1,C1,P1)!=(M2,C2,P2).
     ```
   - **Clue 1:** Hannah paid more than Teri's client.
     - "Hannah" is a client. Let's find the price of the client "Hannah". Let's say `assign(M1, "Hannah", P1)`.
     - "Teri's client" means `assign("Teri", C2, P2)`.
     - Constraint: P1 > P2.
     - ASP: `P1 > P2 :- assign(M1, "Hannah", P1), assign("Teri", C2, P2).`
   - **Clue 2:** Freda paid 20 dollars more than Lynda's client.
     - "Freda" is a client. `assign(M1, "Freda", P1)`.
     - "Lynda's client" means `assign("Lynda", C2, P2)`.
     - Constraint: P1 = P2 + 20.
     - ASP: `P1 = P2 + 20 :- assign(M1, "Freda", P1), assign("Lynda", C2, P2).`
   - **Clue 3:** Hannah paid 10 dollars less than Nancy's client.
     - "Hannah" is a client. `assign(M1, "Hannah", P1)`.
     - "Nancy's client" means `assign("Nancy", C2, P2)`.
     - Constraint: P1 = P2 - 10.
     - ASP: `P1 = P2 - 10 :- assign(M1, "Hannah", P1), assign("Nancy", C2, P2).`
   - **Clue 4:** Nancy's client, Hannah and Ginger were all different clients.
     - This means three distinct clients: the client assigned to Nancy, "Hannah", and "Ginger".
     - So `C_nancy != "Hannah"`, `C_nancy != "Ginger"`, `"Hannah" != "Ginger"` (the last is trivial but we can just state the pairwise inequalities for the first two, or follow the pattern from example 2 which generates pairwise inequalities).
     - Actually, "Hannah" and "Ginger" are constants, so they are already different. We just need to ensure Nancy's client is not Hannah and not Ginger.
     - ASP:
       ```asp
       C1 != "Hannah" :- assign(M, C1, P), M="Nancy".
       C1 != "Ginger" :- assign(M, C1, P), M="Nancy".
       ```
       Or follow the exact pattern from example 2 for "A, B, C are all different":
       ```asp
       C1 != C2 :- assign(M1, C1, P1), assign(M2, C2, P2), M1="Nancy", C2="Hannah".
       C1 != C3 :- assign(M1, C1, P1), assign(M2, C2, P2), M1="Nancy", C2="Ginger".
       ```
       Wait, the clue says "Nancy's client, Hannah and Ginger were all different clients." Since Hannah and Ginger are distinct constants, we just need to ensure Nancy's client is not Hannah and not Ginger. The pairwise inequality pattern in Example 2 is:
       ```asp
       C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
       ```
       That's for three variables. Here we have a fixed constant "Nancy's client" and two constants "Hannah" and "Ginger". So:
       ```asp
       C1 != "Hannah" :- assign(M, C1, P), M="Nancy".
       C1 != "Ginger" :- assign(M, C1, P), M="Nancy".
       ```
       This is sufficient. I'll stick to this simpler form, or adapt to the pairwise style if needed. Actually, the rule `{C1="Hannah"; C1="Ginger"}=0 :- assign(M, C1, P), M="Nancy".` works too. But inequality is fine.

   - **Clue 5:** Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client.
     - "Hannah" is a client. So the client "Hannah" either has price 180, or is the client of Lynda.
     - Let `assign(M, "Hannah", P)` be the assignment for client Hannah.
     - Condition: `P = 180` OR `M = "Lynda"`.
     - ASP: `{P=180; M="Lynda"}=1 :- assign(M, "Hannah", P).`

4.  **Check Consistency & Refine:**
   Let's verify the clues against the ASP formulation.
   - Clue 1: `P1 > P2 :- assign(M1, "Hannah", P1), assign("Teri", C2, P2).` -> Correct.
   - Clue 2: `P1 = P2 + 20 :- assign(M1, "Freda", P1), assign("Lynda", C2, P2).` -> Correct.
   - Clue 3: `P1 = P2 - 10 :- assign(M1, "Hannah", P1), assign("Nancy", C2, P2).` -> Correct.
   - Clue 4: `C != "Hannah" :- assign(M, C, P), M="Nancy".` and `C != "Ginger" :- assign(M, C, P), M="Nancy".` -> Correct. (Hannah and Ginger are distinct by definition, so we only constrain Nancy's client).
   - Clue 5: `{P=180; M="Lynda"}=1 :- assign(M, "Hannah", P).` -> Correct.

   Wait, let's double check Clue 5's wording: "Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client."
   In logic puzzles, "Hannah" refers to the client named Hannah. So the client Hannah has price 180, OR the client Hannah is served by Lynda. Yes, `{P=180; M="Lynda"}=1 :- assign(M, "Hannah", P).` is correct.

   Let's check the prices: 150, 160, 170, 180.
   From Clue 2: Freda's price = Lynda's client's price + 20. Possible pairs: (170, 150), (180, 160).
   From Clue 3: Hannah's price = Nancy's client's price - 10. Possible pairs: (150, 160), (160, 170), (170, 180).
   From Clue 1: Hannah's price > Teri's client's price.
   From Clue 5: Hannah's price is 180 OR Hannah is Lynda's client.

   Let's solve it manually to verify uniqueness and correctness of constraints.
   If Hannah's price is 180:
     - Then from Clue 3, Nancy's client's price = 190. But 190 is not in {150, 160, 170, 180}. Contradiction.
     - Therefore, Hannah's price cannot be 180.
     - So from Clue 5, Hannah MUST be Lynda's client.
     - So `assign("Lynda", "Hannah", P_h)`.
     - From Clue 2: Freda's price = Lynda's client's price + 20 = P_h + 20.
     - Since prices are max 180, P_h + 20 <= 180 => P_h <= 160.
     - Possible P_h: 150 or 160.
     - If P_h = 150: Freda = 170.
       - From Clue 3: Hannah (150) = Nancy's client - 10 => Nancy's client = 160.
       - From Clue 1: Hannah (150) > Teri's client => Teri's client < 150. No price available. Contradiction.
     - If P_h = 160: Freda = 180.
       - From Clue 3: Hannah (160) = Nancy's client - 10 => Nancy's client = 170.
       - From Clue 1: Hannah (160) > Teri's client => Teri's client < 160 => Teri's client = 150.
       - So far:
         - Lynda -> Hannah (160)
         - Freda -> 180
         - Nancy -> 170
         - Teri -> 150
       - Clients assigned: Hannah, Freda, ?, ?. Remaining clients: Aimee, Ginger.
       - Prices assigned: 160, 180, 170, 150. All assigned.
       - Masseuses assigned: Lynda, ?, Nancy, Teri. Remaining: Whitney.
       - So Whitney gets the remaining client/price pair.
       - We have: Lynda-Hannah(160), Freda(180), Nancy(170), Teri(150).
       - Clue 4: Nancy's client, Hannah, and Ginger are different. Nancy's client is 170 (client name unknown yet). It's not Hannah (160) or Ginger. So Nancy's client is either Aimee or Freda or Ginger. But Freda is 180, so Nancy's client is Aimee.
       - So Nancy -> Aimee (170).
       - Then Whitney -> Ginger (180).
       - Check Clue 4: Nancy's client (Aimee) != Hannah, != Ginger. OK.
       - All clues satisfied. Unique solution found.

   The constraints correctly encode the puzzle. The ASP code should generate this unique answer set.

5.  **Construct ASP Code:**
   I will carefully follow the formatting rules.
   - Constants: `client/1`, `price/1`, `masseuse/1`
   - Predicate: `assign/3` (or `match/3`). I'll use `match(M, C, P)` as in examples, or `assign`. The prompt says "Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch)." I'll use `match`.
   - Search space: `{match(M, C, P): client(C), price(P)}=1 :- masseuse(M).`
   - Uniqueness: `{M1=M2; C1=C2; P1=P2}=0 :- match(M1,C1,P1), match(M2,C2,P2), (M1,C1,P1)!=(M2,C2,P2).`
   - Clue 1: `P1 > P2 :- match(M1, "Hannah", P1), match("Teri", C2, P2).`
   - Clue 2: `P1 = P2 + 20 :- match(M1, "Freda", P1), match("Lynda", C2, P2).`
   - Clue 3: `P1 = P2 - 10 :- match(M1, "Hannah", P1), match("Nancy", C2, P2).`
   - Clue 4: `C != "Hannah" :- match(M, C, P), M="Nancy".` and `C != "Ginger" :- match(M, C, P), M="Nancy".`
   - Clue 5: `{P=180; M="Lynda"}=1 :- match(M, "Hannah", P).`
   - Show: `#show match/3.`

   Let's verify the exact syntax for Clue 4. The prompt examples use inequality rules like `C1!=C2 :- ...`. I'll stick to that or use the `{...}=0` pattern. Actually, `C != "Hannah" :- match(M, C, P), M="Nancy".` is perfectly valid ASP. To be safe and consistent with Example 2's style for "all different", I could write:
   ```asp
   C1 != "Hannah" :- match(M, C1, P), M="Nancy".
   C1 != "Ginger" :- match(M, C1, P), M="Nancy".
   ```
   This is fine.

   Wait, check Clue 2: "Freda paid 20 dollars more than Lynda's client." -> `P_freda = P_lynda_client + 20`. My rule: `P1 = P2 + 20 :- match(M1, "Freda", P1), match("Lynda", C2, P2).` Correct.
   Check Clue 3: "Hannah paid 10 dollars less than Nancy's client." -> `P_hannah = P_nancy_client - 10`. My rule: `P1 = P2 - 10 :- match(M1, "Hannah", P1), match("Nancy", C2, P2).` Correct.
   Check Clue 1: "Hannah paid more than Teri's client." -> `P_hannah > P_teri_client`. My rule: `P1 > P2 :- match(M1, "Hannah", P1), match("Teri", C2, P2).` Correct.

   One minor point: In Clue 5, "Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client." This is an exclusive or? "either ... or ..." in logic puzzles usually means inclusive or, but the `{...}=1` enforces exactly one. The phrasing "either A or B" often implies exactly one in these puzzles. I'll use `{P=180; M="Lynda"}=1`. If it were inclusive, it would be `{P=180; M="Lynda"}>=1`. Given puzzle conventions, `=1` is standard. I'll stick with `=1`.

   Let's assemble the full program.

   ```asp
   % Constants
   client("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").
   price(150; 160; 170; 180).
   masseuse("Lynda"; "Nancy"; "Teri"; "Whitney").

   % Search space
   {match(M, C, P): client(C), price(P)}=1 :- masseuse(M).

   % Uniqueness
   {M1=M2; C1=C2; P1=P2}=0 :- match(M1,C1,P1), match(M2,C2,P2), (M1,C1,P1)!=(M2,C2,P2).

   % 1. Hannah paid more than Teri's client.
   P1 > P2 :- match(M1, "Hannah", P1), match("Teri", C2, P2).

   % 2. Freda paid 20 dollars more than Lynda's client.
   P1 = P2 + 20 :- match(M1, "Freda", P1), match("Lynda", C2, P2).

   % 3. Hannah paid 10 dollars less than Nancy's client.
   P1 = P2 - 10 :- match(M1, "Hannah", P1), match("Nancy", C2, P2).

   % 4. Nancy's client, Hannah and Ginger were all different clients.
   C != "Hannah" :- match(M, C, P), M="Nancy".
   C != "Ginger" :- match(M, C, P), M="Nancy".

   % 5. Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client.
   {P=180; M="Lynda"}=1 :- match(M, "Hannah", P).

   #show match/3.
   ```

   Double check constraints format:
   - `price(150; 160; 170; 180).` -> correct.
   - `client("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").` -> correct.
   - `masseuse("Lynda"; "Nancy"; "Teri"; "Whitney").` -> correct.
   - Search space matches example format.
   - Uniqueness matches example format.
   - Clues match example format.
   - Show directive matches.

   One thing: In Clue 4, "Nancy's client, Hannah and Ginger were all different clients." Since Hannah and Ginger are constants, they are inherently different. The constraint just needs to ensure Nancy's client is not Hannah and not Ginger. My rules do exactly that. Alternatively, I could write it as:
   `C1 != C2 :- match(M1, C1, P1), match(M2, C2, P2), M1="Nancy", C2="Hannah".`
   `C1 != C3 :- match(M1, C1, P1), match(M2, C2, P2), M1="Nancy", C2="Ginger".`
   But the simpler `C != "Hannah" :- match(M, C, P), M="Nancy".` is standard and correct. I'll keep it simple.

   Let's verify the "either ... or ..." in Clue 5. Sometimes puzzles use `=` for exclusive or. I'll stick with `=1`.

   All looks solid. I will generate the ASP code now.
   One minor adjustment: I'll format constants exactly as requested: `client("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").` etc.
   I'll ensure the predicate name is descriptive: `match/3` is fine.
   I'll check if any clue needs a different encoding.
   Clue 1: `P1 > P2 :- match(M1, "Hannah", P1), match("Teri", C2, P2).` -> correct.
   Clue 2: `P1 = P2 + 20 :- match(M1, "Freda", P1), match("Lynda", C2, P2).` -> correct.
   Clue 3: `P1 = P2 - 10 :- match(M1, "Hannah", P1), match("Nancy", C2, P2).` -> correct.
   Clue 4: `C != "Hannah" :- match(M, C, P), M="Nancy".` `C != "Ginger" :- match(M, C, P), M="Nancy".` -> correct.
   Clue 5: `{P=180; M="Lynda"}=1 :- match(M, "Hannah", P).` -> correct.

   Everything matches the rules and examples. I will produce the final output.
   One detail: The prompt says "strip special characters ($225 → 225)". I did that.

```asp
% Constants
client("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").
price(150; 160; 170; 180).
masseuse("Lynda"; "Nancy"; "Teri"; "Whitney").

% Search space: for each masseuse M, assign exactly one client C and price P.
{match(M, C, P): client(C), price(P)}=1 :- masseuse(M).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{M1=M2; C1=C2; P1=P2}=0 :- match(M1,C1,P1), match(M2,C2,P2), (M1,C1,P1)!=(M2,C2,P2).

% 1. Hannah paid more than Teri's client.
P1 > P2 :- match(M1, "Hannah", P1), match("Teri", C2, P2).

% 2. Freda paid 20 dollars more than Lynda's client.
P1 = P2 + 20 :- match(M1, "Freda", P1), match("Lynda", C2, P2).

% 3. Hannah paid 10 dollars less than Nancy's client.
P1 = P2 - 10 :- match(M1, "Hannah", P1), match("Nancy", C2, P2).

% 4. Nancy's client, Hannah and Ginger were all different clients.
C != "Hannah" :- match(M, C, P), M="Nancy".
C != "Ginger" :- match(M, C, P), M="Nancy".

% 5. Hannah was either the person who paid $180 or Lynda's client.
{P=180; M="Lynda"}=1 :- match(M, "Hannah", P).

#show match/3.
```

client("Aimee"; "Ginger"; "Freda"; "Hannah").
price(150; 160; 170; 180).
masseuse("Lynda"; "Nancy"; "Teri"; "Whitney").

% Search space: for each masseuse M, assign exactly one client C and price P.
{match(M, C, P): client(C), price(P)}=1 :- masseuse(M).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{M1=M2; C1=C2; P1=P2}=0 :- match(M1,C1,P1), match(M2,C2,P2), (M1,C1,P1)!=(M2,C2,P2).

% 1. Hannah paid more than Teri's client.
P1 > P2 :- match(M1, "Hannah", P1), match("Teri", C2, P2).

% 2. Freda paid 20 dollars more than Lynda's client.
P1 = P2 + 20 :- match(M1, "Freda", P1), match("Lynda", C2, P2).

% 3. Hannah paid 10 dollars less than Nancy's client.
P1 = P2 - 10 :- match(M1, "Hannah", P1), match("Nancy", C2, P2).

% 4. Nancy's client, Hannah and Ginger were all different clients.

C != "Ginger" :- match(M, C, P), M="Nancy".
